scope: PliegoRS G1 native runtime and dynamic rendering source preview
disclaimer: This is a framework ownership map, not an application compliance claim.

controls.scopeNote: Route paths are percent-decoded exactly once before sealed matching; encoded separators, NUL, invalid UTF-8, and second-pass percent forms fail closed.
Typed Pliego DOM rendering applies contextual text and attribute escaping at final serialization.
The framework bounds connections and concurrent requests globally. Per-user and business anti-automation policy remains application and edge ownership.
The reference application demonstrates CSP middleware, but PliegoRS cannot choose a correct application-specific policy automatically.
The reference response-policy middleware emits X-Content-Type-Options. Applications retain response-policy ownership.
The reference response-policy middleware emits Referrer-Policy. Applications retain policy selection.
Core routing and telemetry do not trust forwarding headers. A future proxy-aware host adapter must declare and verify its trust boundary explicitly.
One Hyper parser owns framing. Conflicting Content-Length and Transfer-Encoding are rejected by PliegoRS before the handler, and HTTP/2 uses version-specific framing.
G1 exposes no file-upload or multipart parser. Multipart requests fail closed until G2 defines part, filename, storage, and expanded-size policies.
G1 performs no archive or request decompression. Non-identity Content-Encoding fails closed until an independent decoded-byte budget exists.
Cargo.lock is committed, dependency review is automated, and the release path emits SBOM and provenance artifacts.
Runtime, render, boundary, transport, and shutdown budgets are documented with overload and fixed-load evidence.
Admission, deadlines, backpressure, stream limits, slow-peer timeouts, cancellation, and bounded shutdown are enforced by tests.
G1 keeps route path, query, headers, and body distinct and performs no merged parameter extraction. Typed form and action decoding remain G2 work.
PliegoRS documents its bounded event and OTel field inventory. Destination, retention, access, and alert policy belong to the operator.
Default request logs and OTel signals exclude concrete paths, queries, headers, cookies, bodies, identities, and diagnostic messages.
Lifecycle rejections emit bounded diagnostic codes. Parser-level connection failures remain generic transport events and require operator correlation.
Tracing fields are typed values from bounded enums, sealed route IDs, numeric counters, and finite diagnostic codes; messages and user strings are excluded.
Authored boundaries receive public classes and codes only; built-in failures expose stable codes without stack traces or internal messages.
Middleware capability mismatches, panics, malformed inputs, body limits, and invalid lifecycle transitions fail closed with bounded cleanup.
